claim: ICIMOD's Hindu Kush Himalaya Assessment projects 36-64 percent glacier volume loss by 2100 under high-emission scenarios with accelerated mass loss observed since the 1990s across all major ranges.

source.title: The Hindu Kush Himalaya Assessment: Mountains, Climate Change, Sustainability and People

method: Reviewed executive summary, glacier projection chapters, and water-security implications.
limitations: Projections depend on emission scenarios and model ensembles with substantial spread.

claim: A 2019 Nature study using satellite gravimetry and altimetry found that Himalayan glacier mass loss doubled between 1975-2000 and 2000-2016, with the greatest acceleration in the eastern Himalaya.

source.title: Asia's glaciers are a regionally important buffer against drought

method: Reviewed satellite methodology, mass-loss rates, and regional variation.
limitations: Satellite methods have inherent resolution limits for small glaciers.
Study period ends in 2016 and may not capture recent acceleration.

claim: The ICIMOD Hindu Kush Himalaya Assessment (2019) identifies rising downstream water demand driven by population growth, agricultural expansion, and economic development as a compounding stress on HKH water systems alongside upstream glacier retreat — but does not provide a single regionally-uniform annual demand-growth rate, as the trajectory varies substantially by basin, sector, and country.

source.title: The Hindu Kush Himalaya Assessment: Mountains, Climate Change, Sustainability and People

method: Reviewed water-security and socioeconomic chapters of the HKH Assessment for demand-trend evidence and regional variation across major river basins.
limitations: The HKH Assessment provides narrative and scenario-based demand projections, not a single point-estimate annual growth rate — any specific '2-4 percent' figure would require a basin-specific or sectoral source.
Demand data currency: the assessment synthesises studies up to approximately 2018; demand trajectories may have shifted since.
Basin-level variation is large — Indus, Ganges, Brahmaputra, Mekong, and Yangtze basins have different demand drivers and trajectories.
This record replaces a placeholder that cited the ICIMOD homepage for an unverifiable '2022 study' with a specific but unsupported demand-growth rate.
